artifacts.content:
Hi Em,

Finally sitting down for a minute, Milo is snoring and Ranger is crunching hay outside the window. I realized I never actually told you the whole story about that awful Animal Control visit back on March 18, 2023.

I was halfway through a surgery shift at Front Range when the Larimer County Animal Control officer called and said there’d been an anonymous neglect complaint about “two thin horses” at 4127 Prairie Sky Court. I about threw up. I drove home on my lunch break and she did a full welfare check on Ranger and Daisy in the pens. She body-scored them (5–6), checked feet, hay, water, shelter, the whole nine yards. Then we sat at the tack room table while I pulled Coggins and vaccination records from the clinic system. She closed the case as unfounded the same week, but I still feel shaky thinking about it.

It dredged up all the stuff from Dad’s Chapter 13 last October too—sitting in the Larimer County Courthouse while he agreed to that $620/month payment plan on the Prairie Sky mortgage and promising him I’d pick up extra on‑call shifts at Front Range to help. Between that and Jason’s DUI mess on May 7, 2023, I feel like our address is cursed.

The horses are fine—fat, shiny, slightly annoyed that I keep taking pictures “just in case.” I’ve got detailed feeding/med charts posted in the barn now so if I get yanked away to Larimer County Jail again in the middle of the night for Jason drama, Kelly or Mike can step in without missing a beat.

Anyway, just needed to get that all out of my head. Come ride Daisy this weekend?

Love,
Sarah

metadata.subject: Catching you up on the ACO thing

artifacts.content:
Hey Dad,

I know you hate talking numbers over email, but I wanted to lay everything out so you can look when your brain isn’t fried.

Since the Chapter 13 got finalized at the Larimer County Courthouse in October and the trustee locked in that $620/month payment plan on the Prairie Sky mortgage, I’ve been tracking what I can realistically cover. Right now I’m picking up 8–10 extra on‑call shifts a month at Front Range Mixed Animal. That plus my base salary is enough to throw $300–350 toward your payment without shorting hay or vet stuff here.

I’m still paying off Ranger’s $3,200 colic surgery from CSU Veterinary Teaching Hospital (12‑month plan that runs through October), so I promised myself I wouldn’t cut corners on his meds, rechecks, or the low‑starch feed. Daisy and Finn are staying on their regular farrier and dental schedule too—no skipping cycles to save money. It means I’m tired, but the horses don’t know the difference, which is the point.

I’m also keeping my evening rides at Harrington Ridge and here at home non‑negotiable. After that whole Dr. H mess at the clinic in 2020—when I filed the internal complaint about him skimping on post‑op pain meds and had to talk to the Colorado State Board—I learned that if I drop the horse time, my anxiety spikes fast.

Anyway, I don’t want you worrying that helping with the $620 is wrecking me. It’s tight, but manageable, as long as I keep saying no to random extras (and used trucks that are too shiny).

Love you,
Sarah

metadata.subject: Money, the house, and the horses

artifacts.content:
Journal – 2023-04-22

Trying to make sense of the last few months.

Jason’s DUI on May 7, 2023 still hangs over everything. Those three nights of driving back and forth between Larimer County Jail and Mom and Dad’s in Loveland at 2 a.m., then racing back to 4127 Prairie Sky to throw hay before my 7:30 shift at Front Range, felt like living in a bad TV show. I’m still grateful I had those written feeding and medication charts taped up in the barn so Kelly and Jess could keep Ranger, Daisy, and Finn on their exact schedules when I couldn’t be here.

Layer that on top of Emily’s inpatient stay at Poudre Valley Hospital this spring for severe depression. I’ve been doing 10–12 hour days bouncing between the clinic, PVH, and the barn. Therapist at SummitStone Health Partners keeps reminding me that the horses are part of how I cope, not an extra burden.

It’s wild to think back to January–February 2022, when I went on short‑term disability from Front Range myself and boarded Daisy and Finn out for six weeks while I saw Dr. Karen Lowe at Poudre Valley Behavioral Health. Even then, they were in good weight and up to date on everything, but I was so scared of letting something slip.

Note to self: the pattern is that even when everything else is on fire—Dad’s bankruptcy, Jason’s drinking, Emily’s depression—I still protect the horses’ care. That has to count for something.

Action items: schedule farrier (all three due in 2 weeks), refill Ranger’s gut supplement, book my own therapy follow‑up at SummitStone before show season ramps up.

Event: Evening barn loop – work horses + hospital visit
Date: 2023-03-29
Time: 4:00 p.m. – 10:00 p.m.
Location: Front Range Mixed Animal → Poudre Valley Hospital → Harrington Ridge → Home

Notes:

4:00–5:30 p.m. – Finish up at Front Range Mixed Animal (on call tonight; check with Dr. Patel about any pending large‑animal calls before I leave). Make sure CSU payment portal reminder is set for Ranger’s $3,200 October 2021 colic surgery plan (auto‑draft hits on the 30th).

5:45–7:00 p.m. – Poudre Valley Hospital, 3rd floor behavioral health unit – visit Emily. Bring her sketchbook and the printouts of Milo and Daisy. Try to eat something from the cafeteria so I don’t end up living on granola bars.

7:15–9:00 p.m. – Harrington Ridge Stables, Loveland. Short, focused ride on Finn (20–30 min of flatwork), then 30 min groundwork with Ranger in the outdoor or 60' round pen, depending on wind. Remember how he bounced back after his October 2021 CSU colic surgery—don’t baby him, but don’t overdo it either.

Mental note: If I’m still feeling spun out from Emily’s inpatient stay, use the breathing exercises Dr. Jensen at Summit Counseling walked me through. Horses pick up everything.

9:15–10:00 p.m. – Home at 4127 Prairie Sky Court. Quick check hay/water, top off stock tank heaters, stretch, shower, bed by 10:30 if possible. On call overnight, so phone on loud but face down.

Attendees: just me + horses (Ranger, Daisy, Finn) + Emily at PVH.

Hey Jess,

You asked the other day why I always say yes when people throw their weird/problem horses at me. I was thinking about it on the drive back from Wellington and realized a lot of it goes back to family stuff.

Remember Dad’s wreck at the Larimer County Sheriff’s Posse arena in Loveland on April 3, 2022? Rope horse flipped over on him, three broken ribs, and then the whole DUI citation on top of it because he’d “only had two beers” before hauling in. Once he was home and off pain meds enough to think straight, he flat‑out told me I was the only person he trusted to restart that gelding. So from May through August 2022 I was at their place on North Shields four nights a week doing nothing but groundwork and quiet walk‑trot. His orthopedist at Poudre Valley finally cleared him for 20–30 minutes of riding, but only because the horse was actually safe.

Then there was Jason’s bay gelding last spring, right after his 28‑day inpatient stay at Cedar Springs in Colorado Springs for the depression/alcohol spiral after that bronc‑riding accident. The horse had learned every bolt/spin trick in the Larimer County Fairgrounds pens. I had him at my barn in Fort Collins for about 10 weeks, 4–5 short sessions a week, just rebuilding his brain from the ground up. Dr. Kline finally okayed Jason to ride him again, but only in supervised lessons.

I guess once you’ve watched your dad and your brother both almost get killed by their own horses (and their own choices), it’s hard to walk away from the “difficult” ones.

Anyway, thanks for always handing me a coffee when I drag into the arena looking half‑dead.

– S

metadata.subject: Why I keep saying yes to the tough ones

artifacts.content:
Case/Training Log – Ranger & Facility Use

July 2021 – Clinic mustang quarantine

Front Range took in that newly gathered mustang with suspected strangles exposure. Rather than risk the main barn, Dr. Anika Patel and I set him up here at home in the 60' x 80' pen. Because the pen is fully enclosed with no shared fence lines and outside‑access feeders, we were able to run a full 21‑day isolation protocol without exposing the other clinic horses. Daily temp checks, feed from outside, bleach footbaths at the gate. Good proof that the setup works for biosecurity, not just training.

March 2022 – Emily’s fall off Ranger

Emily came off during a groundwork session in the 50' round pen, landed wrong, and fractured her wrist. Poudre Valley Hospital ER doc actually noted in her chart that the 6' panels, solid barrier along the road side, and perimeter fence likely prevented a loose‑horse incident on North Shields. Scary day, but also validation that the layout does its job when things go sideways.

November 2022 – Animal Control inspection

Neighbor on the corner called Larimer County Animal Control about a “dangerous wild horse” here. Officer Kelly Ramirez walked the round pen and holding pen, checked the 6' pipe panels, double‑chain gates, and visual barriers, and later wrote in her report at the Laporte substation that the facilities exceeded county expectations for unhandled mustangs.

October 2023 – trailer wreck mustang filly from I‑25/Prospect stayed here for post‑concussion neuro checks with Dr. Harris guiding me from Front Range.

Conclusion: as much as I second‑guess myself, multiple vets + ACO have basically signed off on this place.

metadata.title: Note – proof my setup actually works

artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

Just got home from the barn and finally sat down with some tea, so I figured I’d answer your “are you overdoing it?” text properly.

Spring 2023 has been a lot: full‑time at Front Range, twice‑daily trips to Poudre Valley to sit with Emily during her inpatient stay for severe depression, and then trying to keep Ranger and Daisy from turning feral in the meantime. Most nights I’ve been doing 30–40 minutes of hand‑walking, grooming, and a little quiet groundwork at Mike’s place in Wellington after I leave the hospital. It sounds like overkill, but honestly, that routine is what’s keeping me from falling apart.

I’m still seeing the therapist at SummitStone Health Partners once a week. We talked about how similar this feels to when I went on short‑term disability from Front Range in Jan–Feb 2022 and boarded Daisy and Finn out for six weeks while I saw Dr. Karen Lowe at Poudre Valley Behavioral Health. Remember how scared I was that their care would slip? And then they showed up fat, shiny, feet done, vaccines current. The horses were okay; I was the one who needed help.

Same pattern now: even with Emily at PVH and Dad’s bankruptcy payment plan and the fallout from Jason’s DUI, the horses are still getting exactly what they need. I’ve got written charts in the barn so if I get stuck at the hospital late, Kelly can step in.

So yes, I’m tired, but no, I’m not neglecting myself or the animals. I promise I’ll take one real day off once Emily is home and stable.

Love you,
Sarah

From: Michael Harrington <michael@harringtonridgestables.com>
To: Colorado Front Range TIP Challenge Committee <info@cfrtipchallenge.org>
Cc: Sarah Elizabeth McAllister <sarah.e.mcallister.co@gmail.com>
Date: 2026-02-18
Subject: Trainer Reference – Sarah Elizabeth McAllister – Harrington Ridge Stables

To Whom It May Concern,

I’m writing to provide a trainer reference for Sarah Elizabeth McAllister – trainer applicant for your 2026 Colorado Front Range TIP Challenge.

I’ve known Sarah for just over 7 years, since February 2019, when she moved her bay Quarter Horse mare Daisy into my barn at Harrington Ridge Stables in Loveland, CO. She came to us during a really rough stretch for her family: her dad, Mark McAllister, had just gone through a layoff in the 2019 layoff period, and they were working with the bank on a temporary mortgage forbearance on the Fort Collins house at 4127 Prairie Sky Court. That financial strain forced her to rehome another mare she’d been keeping in Greeley, CO, which was heartbreaking for her.

What impressed me from the start was that, even under that pressure, Sarah never cut corners on her horses’ care. Board was always paid on time, she stayed on top of farrier and vet work, and she picked up extra shifts at Front Range Mixed Animal Veterinary Clinic rather than compromise Daisy’s program. She was quiet, clearly exhausted some days, but incredibly consistent.

Over the years, I’ve watched her develop into a thoughtful, fair, and detail-oriented trainer, particularly with mustangs and greener horses. She’s reliable hauling in and out, keeps meticulous ride logs, and is the first to volunteer to help a nervous rider or a difficult horse.

I recommend Sarah without reservation.

Sincerely,

Michael Harrington
Owner/Trainer, Harrington Ridge Stables
Loveland, CO

metadata.recipient: Colorado Front Range TIP Challenge Committee <info@cfrtipchallenge.org>
metadata.subject: Trainer Reference – Sarah Elizabeth McAllister – Harrington Ridge Stables

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Harrington <michael@harringtonridgestables.com>
To: Dr. Karen Lewis <k.lewis@frontrangevetclinic.com>
Date: 2026-03-02
Subject: Re: Character/Trainer Letter for Sarah

Hi Karen,

Happy to write something more personal for Sarah. I’ve known Sarah continuously for a little over 7 years now—since early March 2019, when she brought Daisy to Harrington Ridge Stables in Loveland right after finishing an 8‑week intensive anxiety treatment program at Poudre Valley Hospital in Fort Collins.

At that point she’d been off riding for nearly 3 months. She was very up front with me about needing a quiet, predictable barn routine while she transitioned out of the program and back into normal life. We set up a schedule where she could come out during slower hours, and I watched her rebuild both her confidence and her fitness step by step.

Those first few weeks, her rides were 15–20 minutes of simple walk-trot work and a lot of groundwork. She was religious about sticking to the plan her therapist and the hospital team had suggested: consistent sleep, regular meals, and using the barn time as structured exposure instead of white-knuckling through anxiety spikes. By that summer, you would never have guessed she’d just come out of an 8‑week program.

Since then, she’s been one of the most steady, emotionally regulated trainers I’ve had here. She’s the person I trust with sensitive mustangs, green horses, and nervous owners alike.

Let me know if you need anything more specific.

Best,
Michael

Google Doc – Draft Talking Points for Phone Interview (TIP Challenge Reference Call)
Owner: Michael Harrington (Harrington Ridge Stables)
Last edited: 2026-02-20

• How long I’ve known her
  – First met Sarah Elizabeth McAllister in February 2019 when she moved Daisy to Harrington Ridge Stables, Loveland, CO, from Greeley, CO.
  – Context: her father, Mark McAllister, had just gone through a layoff (2019 layoff period). Family was under serious financial strain, including a temporary mortgage forbearance on their Fort Collins house at 4127 Prairie Sky Court.
  – Because of that, she had to re-home a previous mare in Greeley – still one of the hardest decisions she talks about.
  – Have known her continuously a little over 7 years now.

• Mental health / resilience (only if they ask)
  – Early March 2019: she brought Daisy in right after completing an 8‑week intensive anxiety treatment program at Poudre Valley Hospital in Fort Collins.
  – Nearly 3‑month riding break before that; she was rebuilding from the ground up when she arrived here.
  – Used structured groundwork and short, consistent rides to transition back to regular riding and training.

• Training style
  – Quiet, methodical, very fair to green and mustang-type horses.
  – Keeps detailed written logs of sessions; balances clinic job at Front Range Mixed Animal Veterinary Clinic with barn work.

• Reliability / professionalism
  – Shows up early, stays late, good with owners.
  – Has come through some serious life stress (family finances, mental health) without ever taking it out on the horses.
